text: Strategic vs Principled Nonviolence Understanding the two forms of nonviolence is crucial for realizing the potential of peaceful struggle to revolutionize the social status quo. Strategic nonviolence is considered a tactical approach to radical social change and does not necessarily prescribe adherence to principles that go beyond the action itself. While strategic nonviolence is good at destabilizing authoritarian regimes by utilizing massive civil disobedience with very low costs and thus weakening the government and empowering those who would change it, it fails in the stage of providing good governance. Strategic nonviolence lacks the principle of nonviolence and hence ends up utilizing the tools of the Weberian state to repress the protests which, as we saw in Egypt and Syria, leads to failed revolutions with political developments that are quite poor. Principled nonviolence, as shown by Gandhi and King, is completely different. It provides a philosophy of life which negates violence altogether and is also deeply radical in not accepting the principle of states and Machiavellian politics whatsoever. It believes in synchronizing action and goal in the most emancipating process and in creating culture of peace through a more grassroots development that is empowering and revolutionary. Hence it is essential to have adherence to the principle of nonviolence in order to establish successful revolutions and in order to achieve the full potential of a peaceful revolt.
headline: Fully Human Written
